games.explanation: Bubba Chandler's overall line looks shiny until you notice the league has barely seen him, and the sample that exists tells on him the second a right-handed bat steps in. Marlins righties Stowers and Lopez are exactly the profile that has turned Chandler from a strikeout arm into a batting-practice arm, with the rookie's xwOBA-against jumping to .330 versus same-side hitters. Bachar is no ace, but he is a steadier version of what Pittsburgh is running out there, and the sim has this as a one-run game the market is pricing as a clear Pirates edge. Getting the better team at plus money in a coin-flip park is the entire angle, and +112 is how you collect on it.

Skubal is the best pitcher in the American League and Cleveland is still getting a plus number, which is the entire reason to be here. The board is pricing Detroit like Skubal guarantees a win, but the projected score is a one-run game and the Guardians have the two bats in this matchup who actually scare him, with Ramírez and Hoskins both built to handle elite lefties. Throw in a Detroit lineup leaning hard on Torres against a Cleveland staff that's quietly been one of the league's stingier groups, and you have a coin flip dressed up as a mismatch. +130 on the better team in a tight game is the kind of price you take and stop asking questions.

Nobody wants to back the White Sox against Yamamoto in Los Angeles, which is exactly why the number got this fat. The catch is that Yamamoto is markedly more hittable against righties than lefties, and Chicago's lineup happens to lean right where it matters, with Montgomery and Vargas both posting xwOBAs north of .320 against right-handed pitching. Burke isn't going to silence Ohtani, but the wind blowing in at nine-plus and a neutral park take some of the sting out of the Dodger thump. At +175 you're getting a one-score game priced like a laugher, and the math says it's closer to a coin flip with a tilt.

At +175 you're getting a one-score game priced like a laugher, and the math says it's closer to a coin flip with a tilt.

The board sees Colorado on the road against a better team and assumes the Rockies get steamrolled, which would be a fair read if the Athletics were sending out anyone who could miss a bat. Joey Estes is a contact pinata with a strikeout rate under ten percent and walks to match, which is the exact profile that keeps bad offenses in games they have no business being in. Kyle Freeland is the steadier arm here, and Colorado only needs to hang around to cash this number. At plus the hook for -125, you are buying a coin flip on a game the market has already decided is over.
